summary: In this 1919 interview, Thomas Edison shares his pragmatic approach to invention, emphasizing the necessity of hard work, commercial viability, and rigorous experimentation. He discusses the vast opportunities in electrical engineering, warns against patenting unproven or unwanted ideas, and outlines his vision for future technological needs, including automated housing and renewable energy sources.

principles.statement: Success requires the willingness to pay the price of continuous hard work.
principles.rationale: Many young men fail because they demand shorter hours and more pay without putting in the necessary effort, becoming a drag on their industry.
principles.source_quote: Any boy can be a success, providing he is willing to pay the price, which is continuous hard work.

principles.statement: Reduce an idea to actual practice and ensure it works before applying for a patent.
principles.rationale: Ideas are easy to generate, but developing them into a commercial shape is a long, tedious, and expensive process.
principles.source_quote: I suggest that if the young inventor has an idea he had better reduce it to actual practise and be sure that it works before applying for a patent.

principles.statement: Do not patent anything for which there is no actual demand.
principles.rationale: Obtaining patents for ideas that cannot be commercially exploited is a waste of resources.
principles.source_quote: I have made a rule in my later years, not to patent anything for which I knew there was no actual demand. Merely collecting patents is a waste of time, money, and energy.

frameworks.name: The Invention Process
frameworks.when_to_apply: When setting out to create a new invention or solve a specific problem.
steps: Start with a definite idea of accomplishing a certain result.
Collect all possible data, including scientific and commercial information.
Sketch out every possible and probable way of attaining the desired results.
Carry the plan to success by experimenting and building multiple models, expecting the first to fail.
frameworks.source_quote: I always start out with a definite idea of accomplishing a certain result. I collect all the data possible, both scientific, commercial, and otherwise. I then proceed to sketch out every possible and probable way of attaining results and carry it to success by experimenting. In other words, first plan—then act.

mental_models.name: Disappointments as the "Salt of the Inventor"
mental_models.description: Viewing failures, rejections, or the discovery that an idea is already patented not as a defeat, but as a necessary test of character that ultimately leads to triumph.
mental_models.example: When a young inventor searches the Patent Office and finds their idea is already patented, they must use this disappointment to fuel their resilience and eventual success.

heuristics: Ideas are easy, but working them into commercial shape is generally a long, tedious, and expensive job.
Merely collecting patents is a waste of time, money, and energy.

signature_quotes.text: Any boy can be a success, providing he is willing to pay the price, which is continuous hard work.
signature_quotes.context: When asked about the training and mindset young men need to succeed in electrical engineering.

signature_quotes.text: Ideas are easy, but working them into commercial shape is generally a long, tedious, and expensive job.
signature_quotes.context: Advising young inventors on whether to patent every idea.

signature_quotes.text: I have made a rule in my later years, not to patent anything for which I knew there was no actual demand. Merely collecting patents is a waste of time, money, and energy.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing how many of his 1,400 patents were actually worked.

signature_quotes.text: But disappointments show the salt of the inventor. Only by such disappointments can he triumph finally.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the inevitable setbacks inventors face when searching the Patent Office.

signature_quotes.text: In other words, first plan—then act.
signature_quotes.context: Summarizing his process for perfecting inventions.

anti_patterns.description: Patenting an idea before proving it actually works in practice.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It leads to wasted money and effort because turning a raw idea into a commercial product is tedious and expensive, and the idea might already be patented by someone else.

anti_patterns.description: Collecting patents for inventions that have no market demand.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is a waste of time, money, and energy to protect an idea that cannot be commercially exploited.

anti_patterns.description: Demanding shorter hours and more pay without putting in continuous hard work.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It makes young workers a drag on the industry and prevents them from achieving true success.
